data.title: Tesla Falls Further Behind In The Robotaxi Race As Top Rival Gains Highway Go-Ahead
data.description:
At his company’s April 2019 “Autonomy Day,” Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk predicted that self-driving Tesla vehicles would generate roughly $30,000 a year for their owners, who could deploy them as commercial robotaxis.

Of course, Musk’s prediction was predicated on the arrival of fully proficient advanced self-driving technology, not the FSD (full self-driving) already available in Tesla models that falls short of the capability needed to satisfy regulators and consumers.

Tesla, Musk said, would soon benefit by 25% to 30% of the billions in revenue generated by a million robotaxis. Reactions to Musk in the media and from equity analysts were – to put it mildly -- laced heavy with wonder, hope and abundant skepticism. Six years on, the skeptics still look pretty shrewd. The faith remain so, judging from Tesla’s resilient share price.

Cameras only

Unlike other robotaxi projects that employ cameras, radar, lidar and other software-driven sensing technologies, Tesla and Musk have pursued autonomous driving using mainly cameras alone guided by a massive AI-trained “brain” that makes decisions based on data created from visual images.

Musk, in August, pulled the plug on the company’s much-publicized supercomputer “Dojo” project, which used Tesla’s D1 semiconductor chips to create an autonomous brain based on video based on the driving data of millions of Tesla vehicles. Instead, Tesla has switched to a new chip designed in-house.

Sam Abuelsamid, vice president of market research at the Telemetry communications agency, says “Tesla has claimed since 2016 that cameras are sufficient for full autonomy. It wasn’t true then and it’s not true now.” Abuelsamid, a mechanical engineer, expects Tesla’s projected April 2026 start of robotaxi production “to slip, like so many past predictions by Musk.”

Meanwhile, Alphabet Inc.’s (GOOG) Waymo unit this week announced it is expanding its commercial robotaxi operations to highways and freeways around San Francisco, Los Angeles and Phoenix. The company also operates robotaxis in Atlanta and Austin, reporting an operational level of about 250,000 paid rides a week.

Long history

Waymo, which originated as a research effort of Alphabet’s Google unit more than fifteen years ago, uses a sensing array of 13 cameras, four lidar units, six radar units and an array of external audio units. Tesla vehicles use eight exterior cameras and one interior camera to sense movement and drive conditions.

Tesla’s robotaxis, operating commercially in Austin, so far are in a pilot phase; each vehicle has a safety engineer behind the wheel, available to intervene in emergencies or so-called “edge cases,” unusual circumstances. Compared to Waymo, Tesla appears to be lagging behind and losing ground, though the competition among autonomous providers is far from over.

Robotaxis have the potential to lower the cost of personal transportation, making personal ownership of vehicles less practical and cost efficient – while providing hundreds of billions of new revenue to enterprises that master it first. Beyond Waymo and Tesla, Amazon (AMZN) and the major global automakers each have their own research efforts toward driverless transportation.

Additionally, several Chinese companies are pursuing driverless tech, among them Baidu (BIDU), Pony.ai, DiDi Chuxing (OTCPK:DIDIY), NIO Inc. (NIO), and Geely (OTCPK:GELYF). General Motors Co. (GM) notably shut down its Cruise robotaxi subsidiary in late 2024 following steep losses and clashes with regulators.

No hands, no feet

Tesla has said the robotaxi it calls Cybercab will be a two-seater and won’t have a steering wheel or pedals. Abuelsamid said such a vehicle wasn’t likely to come to market anytime soon because Tesla hasn’t yet won federal regulatory approval, which it requires to sell such a vehicle in the U.S.

Per TechCrunch, Musk’s

“comments on Cybercab (beginning production this April) came just moments after shareholders overwhelmingly approved a compensation package for Musk that could be worth as much as $1 trillion in company shares — the largest in corporate history.”

The $1 trillion stock compensation package awarded to Musk is meant mainly to cement the founder’s control of the company. He no longer sees Tesla as an automotive but rather as a robotics company. Many founders of startups in Silicon Valley lose control once their companies grow large and successful and their original stakes become relatively smaller.

Due to changes in regulation designed to fine incumbent automakers for failing to manufacture battery electrics, Tesla no longer can count on the large cash credits it receives yearly as payment from those automakers in order to reduce fines. In 2023, Tesla received $1.79 billion in regulatory credits and about $11 billion in credits since the rule went into effect.

Another Seeking Alpha analyst reckons that three-quarters of the revenue of Tesla is automotive, which benefits from strong branding as well as a direct distribution model that reduces costs compared to the franchise dealer model common in the U.S. and other markets. About 13% of revenue comes from service and other ancillary activities while the remainder, 12%, derives from Tesla’s energy and storage business.

Given the unpredictability of U.S. politics and regulations, forecasts as to Tesla’s sales are hazy. Quite clear, however, is the growing competition of Chinese BEV manufacturers in Europe and Latin America, where Tesla enjoyed brief dominance and now must fight for every sale.

A bet on Tesla is fundamentally a bet on Musk to adapt to growing competition from other developers of autonomous technology as well as the rising tide of BEV manufacturers from China. Shutdown of the Dojo project and a change of strategy suggests Tesla’s autonomous driving capability robotaxi ambitions – Musk’s perpetual optimism notwithstanding – have a way to go.

In the short term, U.S. automakers will continue to introduce new, improved and more cost efficient BEV models, while concentrating on gas-electric hybrids to reduce CO2 emission. To buy Tesla shares an investor must have strong faith in Musk to keep overcoming long odds and maintain a long-term perspective on the company. My Hold recommendation means those already in might as well stay in – and those on the sidelines should stay there.

data.title: Charter Communications: Broadband Collapse Fears Are Overblown
data.description:
Shares of Charter Communications (CHTR) have been a very poor performer over the past year, losing roughly half of their value. We have seen increasing competition for broadband subscribers, which has created a wave of profound pessimism around the cable stocks, as internet demand is key to offsetting structural pressure in the video business. I last covered shares of Charter in July, when I upgraded the stock to a “Strong Buy” given my view that pessimism had reached an extreme. This was a terrible call, with the stock losing another 35% as fears have only built. Given that, now is a good time to reevaluate CHTR.

Shares have been weighed down by weakness in broadband, and this pressure was evident in its most recent results. In the company’s third quarter, reported on Oct. 31, Charter earned $8.34 per share, which was $0.95 below expectations as revenue fell by 1% to $13.7 billion. The company generated $5.6 billion of EBITDA, down 1.5% from last year. Lower advertising spending drove the decline. Advertising on its local stations was seasonally higher last year given the presidential election. Political spending is a meaningful driver of local ad revenue, and that naturally falls during an off-cycle year. Advertising fell nearly $100 million to $356 million. Absent this, revenue and EBITDA were basically flat.

The problems really are on the broadband side of the business. In Q3, Charter lost 109,000 internet customers. Over the past year, subscribers are down about 1.5%. Despite a lower user count, internet revenue grew by 1.7% to $6 billion, given price increases. Still, the pace of losses has been disappointing, and that may also limit future pricing power. While churn on its existing customer base in the internet has been fairly stable, gross adds have been running weaker as competition has intensified. Gradually, we are seeing competition from nontraditional players, be it Starlink or the wireless companies.

For a long time, the view on the sector was that internet gains could offset video losses. After all, flipping to streaming requires a strong internet connection, and daily life is only becoming increasingly online-centric. Internet usage is in no way declining, and it is difficult to imagine usage falling. The problem is that more players are competing for customers. Plus, weak activity in the housing market has reduced the pool of potential new customers, as when someone moves, that is a prime opportunity to get them to switch. Broadband gross adds are likely to stay suppressed as a result.

While the weakness in broadband is getting much of the focus, there are other mechanics in the business that are quite interesting to me. Charter lost 64,000 video customers in Q3. It has cut the pace of video losses by about 70% over the past year. Frankly, it is a shocking result compared to a couple of years ago to see the company lose more broadband than video customers. Video revenue fell by 9% to $3.4 billion, worse than the 3.5% drop in subscribers, as consumers are also trading down to cheaper packages. Still, with the pace of subscriber losses lessening, I expect the revenue declines to moderate.

The improvement (from bad to less bad) on video is encouraging, and management’s ability to moderate declines should provide some support for its broadband over time. The company is not helpless to trends; it can invest in the product offerings to improve competitiveness. We are seeing that in the video.

To some extent, the business may be benefiting from the fact that most of the consumers who want to cut the cord already have, and those who are left are more committed to the package. However, CHTR also has increased streaming offerings as part of its bundle, which may be helping to stabilize its customer base, as it becomes more cost-effective to buy a cable package with streaming apps bundled in than buying these apps directly.

On the earnings call, management reiterated a goal to grow video customers again post-Cox. A couple of years ago, such a goal would have seemed wildly unrealistic. However, we have seen marked improvement in the pace of decline. I still view a return to sustained growth as unlikely and a highly ambitious target. However, to the extent this business is a “melting ice cube,” the pace of the melt is clearly slowing, driving incremental free cash flow. With gradual price increases, we should be able to see video EBITDA stabilize over the medium term.

Elsewhere, its mobile unit remains a bright spot. Mobile revenue was up 19% to $954 million, a bit slower than the 22% growth in lines, as new subscribers often get the first year at a discount. This unit added 493k lines, bringing the business to 11.4 million customers. Critically, as the mobile unit grows and develops scale, we are seeing margins steadily improve. The mobile offering is not only helping to reduce churn at its other products but is also generating cash flow on a stand-alone basis, with margins doubling over the past three years to 34%. I expect ongoing mobile growth, though the pace will slow given the higher installed user base, and this product is a key driver of potential free cash flow growth over the medium term.

While broadband is disappointing, video is getting less bad, and mobile does remain a source of strength. Apart from the nuances in political advertising, this business is basically stable—not really growing but also not shrinking. At the same time, it is highly cash-generative. In Q3, Charter generated $1.6 billion of free cash flow, similar to last year. This comes even as it spent $3 billion on cap-ex in Q3, and we should see spending gradually fall over the next few years as it completes network upgrades.

Cap-ex is now expected to be $11.5 billion this year, as about $500 million of spending was pushed into next year. We should see a modest decline in spending next year. Changes to tax laws this year, namely the immediate expensing of cap-ex for tax purposes, should meaningfully reduce the company’s cash taxes and improve free cash flow, given its large cap-ex budget. These two factors should support nearly $4 billion of incremental free cash flow at the same level of EBITDA over the next 2-3 years. I would note that I expect cap-ex to run $1-1.5 billion higher than this.

The company ended the quarter with $95 billion in debt at a 5.2% average rate. Leverage stands at 4.2x. This debt load has been an increasing focus as EBITDA has been under pressure, given the company’s focus on share buybacks rather than debt reduction. Once it completes its Cox acquisition, it is targeting 3.5-4x leverage, which will take 2-3 years to achieve. I think it is important to note that even while buying back stock and facing broadband challenges, leverage has been basically stable.

Just given the magnitude of its debt load, Charter has significant debt maturities in most years, though it has largely handled 2026 maturities. 2028-2032 are its peak maturity years, and if interest rates are higher, that could decrease cash flow gradually. Of course, the rate environment 4 years from now is unknowable, and total interest expense risk is less than $500 million.

In an environment where EBITDA is steadily declining, its debt load would be a greater concern, and CHTR would need to steadily repay debt to maintain leverage. However, in a stable EBITDA environment, it can maintain the current level of debt, rolling over maturities while keeping leverage flat. Plus, its lower cap-ex needs to provide incremental cash that can be used for debt reduction while still maintaining the same pace of buybacks. Indeed, Charter continues to aggressively repurchase stock, though that has not provided much support for shares. Q3 buybacks totaled $2.2 billion. As a result, its share count is down 6% over the past year.

Frankly, I support Charter’s capital allocation framework, and I believe its debt load has received outsized focus, given leverage is reasonable. With shares at 6x free cash flow, I believe buybacks, not debt reduction, are the best use of cash flow, and with shares so low, we should see even faster share count reduction.

To me, valuation in Charter has become very disjointed. Back in July I was targeting $34 of run-rate free cash flow once cap-ex reaches its normalized $9 billion level. This assumed 0.5% weaker annual internet net adds. With incremental tax law benefits and share count reduction, it may be closer to $40. At ~6x free cash flow, the market is pricing in a ~10% long-term decline in free cash flow. This basically implies no mobile growth and internet that performs as poorly as video has been. While broadband has faced more competitive pressure, I struggle to see a video-like outcome as likely, given the demand for data. Plus, we are actually now seeing signs of improvement in video, suggesting the worst may be over.

I have been far too early on Charter, but with stable EBITDA, a sustainable long-term debt load, and free cash flow flexing higher, this is an attractive value play. Sentiment is so negative that upside is probably not imminent, but this is one of, if not the cheapest stocks I follow. I am not backing off my "Strong Buy" view and expect long-term investors to benefit from buying CHTR here.

data.title: Arista's Bull Trap Plays Out, Networking Prospects Remain Robust
data.description:
I previously covered Arista Networks Inc. (NYSE:ANET) (ANET:CA) in October 2025, discussing how the OpenAI's (OPENAI) mega deals had buoyed the ongoing AI mania, with the former likely to benefit thanks to the growing AI networking demand and the highly strategic partnership with Broadcom (AVGO).

Despite the tailwinds arising from two key customers' higher data-center-related capex trends and the potential outperformance in the FQ3'25 earnings call, I had rated the stock as a Hold then, attributed to the premium valuations and the potentially unsustainable upward momentum.

In this article, I shall discuss ANET's robust prospects arising from the growing hyperscaler demand for high speed networking, as two of its customers guide higher FY2026 data center capex and more hyperscalers adopt 800GbE data center Etherlink switches.

Given the improved margin of safety arising from the recent AI-related market-wide meltdown and the robust performance metrics, ANET is a lot more compelling now - with it warranting an upgraded Buy rating.

Demand Grows For High Speed Networking

ANET 1Y Stock Price

For now, ANET has indeed pulled back by -25.1% since the 52 week heights, with it lending credence to my prior caution and a similar correction also observed in its semiconductor peers in varying degrees.

This is also why I have previously cautioned against chasing overpriced stocks, attributed to the potential volatility once the AI exuberance is moderated.

Otherwise, investors can not deny ANET's ability to capitalize on the ongoing data center boom, as observed in the elevated market share in the 100G/200G/400G/800G Ethernet Port at 43% in FY2024 (-2.4 points YoY, quarterly data not available) against Cisco's (CSCO) moderating share at 13.6% (-6.1 points YoY).

Data Center Etherlink Switch Projections

Moving forward, I am of the opinion that ANET may also benefit from the increased hyperscaler demand for higher capacity/speed networking offerings, at a time when "hundreds of thousands of GPUs" and multi-data center training/inferencing capabilities are seemingly becoming the norm.

My optimism arises from the fact that industry-wide 800GbE port shipments have "more than tripling sequentially in Q2'25," with market analysts already looking beyond the 800 GbE to the next-gen 1.6 Tbps opportunities through 2029.

These reasons may also be why ANET has recently launched 800 Gbps R4 system to be used with AVGO's HyperPort, allowing them to deliver a notably higher data speed at 3.2 Tbps while reducing the total cost of ownership.

This is on top of HyperPort's ability to scale "GPU clusters across multiple buildings... over extended distances that ensures high-performance, efficient and reliable service delivery," with it expected to deliver "70% performance increase and a 40% reduction in job completion times (even under oversubscribed conditions). This directly translates to faster AI model training and enhances system efficiency."

If anything, recent trends have hinted at data center clusters growing even larger, as similarly observed in Amazon AWS' (AMZN) latest data center, Project Rainier delivering "nearly half a million Trainium2 chips in record time, with Anthropic scaling to more than one million chips by the end of 2025."

With AMD's CEO (AMD) also projecting a doubled 2030 AI market size from $500B to $1T in the recent Analyst Day, I am of the opinion that ANET is likely to remain a great beneficiary of the multi-year cloud super cycle as the latter monetizes its next gen higher capacity/speed networking products.

A similar development has been observed in Crehan Research's projection for the 800GbE market, with the segment to experience "projected 90% five-year average annual growth rate driven by AI, storage, and general compute workloads."

Ethernet Market Size Forecast

At the same time, readers may want to note that the open ecosystem Ethernet networking offerings are expected to grow in market share against Nvidia's (NVDA) InfiniBand offerings over the next few years, with it likely to benefit ANET as a leading Ethernet player as more hyperscalers scale out their data centers.

This is especially since ANET's two key customers, Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta (META) have already guided higher data center capex from 2026 onwards, with it likely to drive the former's top/bottom-line growth prospects over the next few years.

While the increased Etherlink adoption by neoclouds/hyperscalers has already triggered its lumpy gross margins as similarly observed in the FQ3'25 numbers of 65.2% (-0.4 points QoQ/+0.6 YoY/+0.5 from FY2019 levels of 64.7%), I am not overly concerned indeed, attributed to the higher sales volumes and the growing deferred revenue (backlog) by +121.3% YoY:

But be that as it may, we do have a mix of product margin where it's significantly below 60% with our cloud and AI titans driving the volume and higher obviously, for the enterprise customers. The average of which, together with services is yielding that number. So when the mix tilts heavily towards the cloud and AI, you can expect some pressure on our gross margins. (Seeking Alpha)

ANET's AI Monetization Remains Robust

For now, ANET's ability to capitalize on the ongoing AI/data-center boom has been exemplified by the double beat FQ3'25 performance as posited in my last article, with FQ3'25 revenues of $2.3B (+27.4% YoY) and adj EPS of $0.75 (+25% YoY after the 4-for-1 stock split).

Thanks to MSFT's and META's promising guidance, I can understand why ANET has also offered a relatively promising FY2026 revenue guidance of $10.65B (+20.2% YoY, based on the YTD performance at $6.51B and the FQ4'25 guidance at $2.35B at the midpoint).

Despite the supposedly mixed FY2026 top-line growth prospects against FY2025's guidance at +26.5% YoY, FY2024 levels of +19.5% YoY, and FY2023 levels at +33.8% YoY, I am of the opinion that ANET's networking outlook remains robust, since most of its Scale-Up Ethernet opportunities will only "be underway in 2026 and really emerge in 2027."

This is on top of ANET highlighting the maturing top-line growth profile of "the existing business, which is already on very large numbers, will have lesser growth," with it exemplifying "the law of large numbers ... that a large entity that is growing rapidly can't maintain that growth pace forever."

Despite the tougher YoY comparison observed in its top-lines, ANET continues to report robust adj EPS growth at +31.4% on a YTD basis as well, compared to FY2024 levels of +31.2% YoY and FY2023 at +51.5% YoY, with it underscoring its consistently profitable growth prospects ahead.

ANET Valuations

Thanks to the recent correction, ANET looks a lot more compelling at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 44.22x as well, down from the recent heights of 54.13x while nearing the 1Y mean of 42.03x and the 5Y mean of 36.11x.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

The same has been observed in my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.05x, based on FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 44.22x and the consensus 3Y adj EPS growth estimates of +21.5%, nearer to the 5Y mean of 2.00x, with it apparent that the correction has been a boon for those whom has been patient.

ANET Is Upgraded To A Buy

ANET 5Y Stock Price

For now, ANET has already returned much of its August/September/October 2025 gains, with the stock also retesting the Q3'25 support levels of $120s by the time of writing.

ANET Technical Indicators

A similar trend has been observed in ANET's technical indicators, with the RSIs already entering oversold levels and the trading volumes notably moderating from prior heights, with it naturally lending credence to my last two Hold ratings.

Based on the LTM adj EPS performance at $2.78 (+29.3% sequentially) and the 1Y P/E mean of 42.03x (nearer to the peer group mean of ~38.70x), it is apparent that the correction has already brought the stock nearer to my fair value estimates of $116.80.

Based on the stock prices of $123.45 by the time of writing, there is also an expanded upside potential of +38.5% to my long-term price target of $171.00, based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $4.07.

As a result of the improved margin of safety arising from the ongoing AI-related correction and its robust networking prospects, I am upgrading the ANET stock to a Buy here.

data.title: NICE: Why I See A Contrarian AI Buy Hiding Inside This Selloff
data.description:
Thesis

Right now, NICE Ltd. (NASDAQ:NICE) might be one of those enterprise-software companies the market just doesn’t fully appreciate. The company is based in Israel and was founded back in the 1980s. Today, NICE sells AI-powered cloud platforms for customer engagement and for financial crime and compliance. Its core products include the CXone contact center platform and its Actimize solutions, which are used by banks, large companies, and public agencies around the world.

With the stock down about 20% in just five days and almost 40% so far this year, the comment sections on SA show how split investors are. Some short-term traders are furious, calling it a terrible company and blaming the guidance, while others argue the drop is an overreaction. After going over the Capital Markets Day transcript, I came to the conclusion that the accelerated selloff in NICE’s share price has more to do with how management framed its near-term investment binge than with any real deterioration in the underlying business.

Margin Reset Ahead

Imagine you’ve been invested in a company for years, and it has a very dependable personality. It usually earns a lot of profit on every dollar it brings in, and it turns a big chunk of that profit into real cash. For NICE, that meant A-grade margins and FCF margin. I’d imagine that investors came to expect that and many bought the stock specifically because it behaved like a “cash machine” that just kept compounding value over time.

At the Capital Markets Day, NICE basically told investors that its normally predictable behavior is going to shift for a while. The company explained that next year it plans to make a big round of investments to go deeper into AI, fold Cognigy into the business, grow its global reach, and build the cloud infrastructure needed to support all of that. These investments don’t turn into revenue right away, they first show up as expenses. And when expenses rise faster than revenue, profit margins go down.

Management is telling investors to expect the operating margin to fall from about 31% in 2025 to around 25–26% in 2026 and they also expect the free-cash-flow margin to drop to about 18–19% in 2026. NICE didn’t present this as a short-term cost-cutting phase either.

Instead, CEO Scott Russell said they’re “in growth mode” and are “unapologetically” spending more right now because they think this is their chance to secure long-term leadership in AI. For longtime shareholders, funds, and the rest, I can only imagine those figures look like a clear drop from what they’ve been used to seeing.

Spending Surges, Growth Doesn’t

According to NICE, they said they plan to spend about $160 million more than usual on operating costs and of that amount, $95 million will be used for things like cloud systems, AI engineering work, and building new products. Meanwhile, the other $65 million will be used to grow their sales teams, build partnerships, and support other go-to-market activities. That’s a very large increase in spending for just one year.

When a company spends that much money, personally, I naturally want to know how quickly revenue will grow as a result. And that’s where the tension comes in. NICE’s own forecast says total revenue in 2025 will lock in only 7% to 8%, and in 2026 it’s expected to grow just 7% to 9%. They said that the real improvement doesn’t show up until 2028, when management says total revenue should finally grow 12% to 14%, with cloud revenue rising 17% to 19%.

From what I read, NICE spent a big portion of the event hyping the sweeping possibilities of AI, automation, and long-term transformation. Here are just of few quotes from CEO Russell, spotlighting AI, Automation, and long-term transformation:

  • AI is a tailwind for NICE for this market. I'm talking about the market. It is a tailwind. There is no doubt.
  • This is a massive market. The AI potential, the transformation that it brings, the opportunity it brings in CX has got a whole lot better.
  • While we are incredibly bullish and we are, we are incredibly bullish about the opportunity that AI brings to be able to automate low-complexity transactions.
  • And the ability to go into the mid and back office, we will more than double our total addressable market within the next three years. This is a great market of which we are well positioned to capitalize on.
  • In fact, I'll tell you this. We are going to aggressively go after every, every, every company. We're going to be explicit to go after everyone that uses another CCaaS platform because what a great opportunity for us with the richness of knowledge that we have to be able to have an embedded…

That’s nice. But the short-term numbers barely move. I think it’s reasonable to say that the gap between the big long-term vision and the modest near-term growth is exactly the kind of thing that makes investors head for the exits.

Cognigy’s Long Road to Contribution

If you remember, NICE spent almost one billion dollars to buy Cognigy, and even though management indicated this purchase was strategically very important, they made it clear it wasn’t expected to give the business an immediate financial boost.

At the event, CFO Beth Gaspich stressed that Cognigy will need a lot more investment. And that brings us back to the spending on cloud delivery, AI engineering, R&D, and global go-to-market efforts, all happening before Cognigy's expected to make any meaningful contribution to the company’s results.

My concern is that NICE is baking in what the current tech can do right now. Today. But things are moving fast, and I don't think it’s hard to picture some under-the-radar breakthrough popping up next year, never mind two years from now, making all this extra spending look pointless. So it’s reasonable to worry about ramping up even more costs, on top of Cognigy’s already heavy price tag, just to stay in the AI race. Gaspich flagged that possibility. She said:

For R&D, we purchased Cognigy… we plan to fuel it further. So we are going to continue to invest in Cognigy as well as our AI roadmap for our other AI capabilities at NICE.

Gaspich added:

We are going to bring in tools and more focus on subject matter experts as part of our go-to-market that will also allow us to seize this opportunity. So this is all incremental revenue spend on top of what you would typically see for us.

Management did lay out Cognigy’s expected financial contribution, but they did it with a lot of caution. Gaspich put the boost to cloud revenue at roughly 150 to 250 basis points a year. That’s fine as far as it goes, but it’s not the kind of step-change some investors probably thought they were getting for a near–billion-dollar deal.

Big AI Story, Small AI Share

By now, we all get that AI is this huge, life-changing breakthrough, and that it’s a market that’s growing extremely fast and a technology that will reshape the future. It's a message that matches what we’re hearing from the rest of the industry and the exact forecasts differ depending on who you ask, but they all point to growth measured in the trillions of dollars.

Gartner, for example, now thinks global AI spending will go over $2 trillion in 2026 and describes AI as one of the main reasons IT budgets are growing. IDC’s latest five-year forecast also says AI IT spending is expected to grow at about a 31.9% compound rate from this year to 2029 and could reach around $1.3 trillion dollars by 2029, with agentic AI called out as a major driver. And according to McKinsey, they calculated that almost 90% of organizations use AI in at least one part of their business.

When it comes to NICE, CFO Gaspich made it very clear that in 2025 the company expects about $2.2 billion dollars in cloud revenue, and only about 12% of that will come from AI. In other words, almost 88% of their cloud business is still standard, non-AI software.

And looking further ahead, management set a goal of $1 billion in AI revenue by 2028. But there’s important context: by 2028 they expect total cloud revenue to be about $3.5 billion. So even then, AI would make up roughly 35% of the cloud business, a real jump, but not the sudden takeover some investors might have assumed based on the tone of the event.

NICE’s Valuation

At around $105 a share, the stock trades at a non-GAAP P/E of 8.75 based on the last year’s results and 8.61 based on upcoming estimates. That’s far below its own five-year average in the mid-20s and the sector’s average in the low-20s.

The EV/EBIT numbers show the same thing: about 10.1x on trailing EBIT and 7.0x on forward EBIT, compared with its historical levels above 30x and a sector median in the mid-20s.

EV/EBITDA is also cheap at 7.8x trailing and 6.2x forward, less than half the sector’s typical 17–18x. In other words, the market has already shifted NICE’s valuation from that of a high-quality compounder to something more like a no-growth cyclical stock.

Meanwhile, cash flow shows the business is stronger than the market’s current label suggests considering that over the last twelve months, NICE produced $786 million in operating cash flow and spent only $23 million on capex, leaving about $587 million in levered free cash flow. That works out to $11.02 of free cash flow per share, which gives me a FCF yield a little above 10% at today’s price. Five years ago, this stock didn’t offer anything close to that kind of yield. And even though free cash flow fell 20% last year, the longer-term trends in both free cash flow and net income are still clearly rising, and management is guiding long-term EPS and FCF growth in the high single digits.

And with management’s plan to ramp up operating costs next year, even if free cash flow takes a hit during that transition, the company still has a big enough cushion to get through it without straining the balance sheet, cutting growth plans, or putting liquidity at risk. I think that the risk is that the market isn’t entirely overreacting right now, it may just be pricing in a slower and bumpier period ahead.

Wall Street expects EPS to grow about 10% to wrap up the year, drop almost 6% in 2026, then pick back up with 13% growth in 2027 and 26% in 2028, all while management is intentionally squeezing margins to pay for AI and cloud investments. So if that rebound in growth doesn’t happen, then an 8–9x earnings multiple and a 10% FCF yield might simply be the right price for a slower, more competitive version of NICE.

Final Takeaway

I'm rating NICE as a Buy. The overall mood in tech has been sharply bearish, oddly well-timed and arguably pushed along by Michael Burry of The Big Short. Even so, with regard to NICE, I think the market's pricing in too much worry about a temporary margin drop in 2026 and not giving enough credit to a business that’s still profitable, generates solid cash, and can grow EPS and FCF at a high single-digit rate over the long run. Sometimes selloffs are a gift. This looks like one of them.

data.title: Ohio Valley Banc Corp: Strong Despite Non-Recurring Items
data.description:
Introduction

Ohio Valley Banc Corp (OVBC) is the holding company for the Ohio Valley Bank with about two dozen offices focusing on the southeastern part of Ohio as well as western West Virginia. It’s a small bank with just under $1.6B in assets on the balance sheet, and in this article I wanted to have a look at its recent earnings report and the quality of its loan book.

The results: look beyond the non-recurring items

In the third quarter of the current financial year, Ohio Valley Banc reported a double digit percentage increase in its interest income which jumped to $21.5M while the total amount of interest expenses stagnated (those expenses increased by just over 1%). As you can see below, this results in a net interest income of approximately $14.6M.

The bank mainly relies on the net interest income as it recorded a net non-interest expense of almost $10M in the third quarter. That being said, we need to highlight the$1.22M loss on the sale of securities which of course had a very noticeable impact on the bottom line result. The provision for loan losses also remains relatively elevated at $1.1M and this resulted in a reported pre-tax income of approximately $3.7M and a net profit of $3M for an EPS of $0.64.

Keep in mind that without the non-recurring loss related to the sale of securities the net profit likely would have been quite a bit higher, at around $0.84-0.85 per share. The EPS in the first nine months of the year was $2.47, and this also includes the impact of the $1.22M loss on the sale of securities as well as the $2.7M in loan loss provisions (which is almost 50% higher than in the first nine months of last year. I will discuss the loan book later in this article).

OVBC currently pays a dividend of $0.23, and based on the Q3 and 9M 2025 results, that dividend is quite well covered as the payout ratio is just over 40% based on the Q3 result, which included the non-recurring items.

A closer look at the loan book

At the end of the third quarter, Ohio Valley Banc Corp had just under $90M in cash and cash equivalents while it also had about $267M in securities (of which approximately 98% are securities available for sale). The total size of the loan book is $1.13B, and $1.12B after taking the accumulated loan loss provisions into account.

As you can see below, about 35% of the loan book consists of residential real estate but commercial real estate remains the most important element in the loan book as it accounts for about 30% while construction-related CRE loans represent another 7% of the loan book. And as shown below, the bank has a relatively high exposure (as a percentage of the total loan book) to consumer loans.

It’s of course important to keep an eye on the quality of the loan book. At the end of the third quarter, almost $21M of the loans was classified as ‘past due’ and that’s about 50% higher than at the end of last year, and that increase is mainly related to the construction loans where none of the loans were past due at year-end 2024 while a $5.9M loan is now classified as ‘between 30 and 59 days past due’ at the end of Q3.

It is encouraging to see that in excess of half of the loans that are classified as past due are backed by real estate assets as collateral, and OVBC should be able to avoid any big haircuts on that type of loans. It’s also interesting to see that the majority of the loans past due are in the 30-59 day segment. This indicates that hopefully the borrower can still rectify the situation, but in a worst case scenario, this could indicate the financial strength of the borrowers has deteriorated (perhaps by the slowing economy in the USA and/or the impact of tariffs which make it more difficult to do business.

Investment thesis

The realized loss on the sale of securities is unfortunate, and the higher loan loss provisions also weigh on the bottom line result in the third quarter. Although I will have to keep an eye on the quality of the loan book, the increased provisions are related to the loan book expansion and the updated GDP and unemployment projections.

I think the fourth quarter will be an important quarter as A) we should see the profitability being restored as losses on the sale of securities should be non-recurring and B) we should hopefully see an improvement in the net interest margins which should ultimately lead to a quarterly result getting closer to $1/share. At the end of the third quarter, the tangible book value came in at just over $33/share, and I am more than happy to pay a small premium over the TBV for a bank trading at a high single digit earnings multiple.

I currently have no position in OVBC but I will start following the bank more closely.

data.title: Amazon's Breakout Is Here - Outsized AWS & Advertising Performance
data.description:
I previously covered Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), (AMZN:CA) in August 2025, discussing how its ability to monetize the ongoing AI boom had been undisputed, as observed in AWS' richer LTM operating margins and the growing multi-year backlog.

Combined with the growing advertising opportunities arising from the growth flywheel across commerce, the Prime subscription/streaming, and the logistics capabilities, I had believed that the stock's wide moat remained highly compelling - resulting in my reiterated Buy rating upon a moderate dip then.

In this article, I shall discuss AMZN's recent outperformance in the FQ3'25 earnings call across the AWS and advertising segments, with it underscoring the company's highly successful monetization cadence beyond commerce.

Despite the break out post FQ3'25 earnings call, I am of the opinion that the stock remains a compelling Buy for those looking to tap into the multi-year cloud super cycle and the secular advertising growth in the streaming end markets.

Renewed Monetization Opportunities Beyond eCommerce

AMZN 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Buy article, AMZN has finally broken out of its sideways trading cadence since early January 2025, with the stock also charting new heights of $250s by early November 2025.

Much of its tailwinds are attributed to the sandbagged FQ3'25 guidance, the consequent double beat FQ3'25 performance, and the promising FQ4'25 guidance.

These underscore how AMZN has been able to drive profitable growth across the outsized AWS and advertising performance, with the impacted North American operating incomes merely attributed to one-time legal settlement with the Federal Trade Commission.

1. AWS/Trainium

AWS has delivered on its undisputed cloud leading position with 29% in market share in Q3'25, through the accelerated revenue growth to $33B in FQ3'25 (+6.8% QoQ/+20.2% YoY) despite the tougher YoY comparison from a year ago at +19% YoY - with it being "our largest growth rate in 11 quarters."

This is especially since Trainium2 has been seeing "strong adoption, is fully subscribed is now a multibillion-dollar business that grew 150% quarter-over-quarter," as AMZN previously "gone back to our manufacturing partners multiple times to produce much more than we’d originally planned.”

Despite AMZN previously offering Trainium2's "same computing power as Nvidia’s H100 chips at 25% of the price," it is apparent that the hyperscaler is still able to generate profitable cloud operations thus far, as observed in the richer LTM operating margins of 35.9% (+0.6 points sequentially/+9.7 from FY2019 levels of 26.2%).

These developments reinforce the fact that AWS remains AMZN's bottom-line driver while lending credence to the management's aggressive data center/custom AI accelerator investments thus far.

With Project Rainier coming online by October 29, 2025, I am of the opinion that AWS is likely to report outsized FQ4'25 performance as the company monetizes the growing capacity.

This is while delivering (part) of the growing multi-year remaining performance obligation of $200B in FQ3'25 (+2.5% QoQ/+21.9% YoY), with it lending great insights to AWS' long-term growth prospects, as the management also hints at outsized FQ4'25 backlog growth in the recent earnings call:

Backlog grew to $200 billion by Q3 quarter end and doesn't include several unannounced new deals in October, which together are more than our total deal volume for all of Q3. AWS is gaining momentum. (Seeking Alpha)

Combined with the projected doubling in AWS capacity by 2027, it goes without saying that AMZN may remain a beneficiary of the ongoing cloud boom.

2. Advertising

Moving on to the next growth driver, AMZN's advertising.

Despite the nascency compared to the commerce and AWS segment, I have observed an accelerating growth rate in its advertising revenues to +22% YoY (excluding FX) in FQ3'25, compared to a year ago at +19% YoY.

When compared to its direct peers, Google (GOOG) at +12.6% YoY/+10.4% YoY and Meta (META) at +25.5% YoY/+18.5% YoY, respectively, it is apparent that AMZN may eventually be a force to be reckoned with in the advertising segment while potentially challenging the current duopoly.

Growing CTV Advertising Opportunities

My optimism arises from AMZN Demand-Side Platform's [DSP] highly strategic partnerships with other CTV players, including The Walt Disney Company (DIS), Roku, Inc. (ROKU), and Netflix (NFLX).

This strategy has directly triggered AMZN's expanded exposure to the CTV advertising end market, since these four streaming platforms (including Prime Video) comprise 19.5% of the total TV viewing share and 43.1% of the total streaming share as of September 2025.

This is significantly aided by AMZN's doubled ad load on Prime video to "4 to 6 minutes of ads per hour, up from 2 to 3.5" in early 2024, with it implying the growing advertiser base and the higher demand for its ad inventory, thanks to Prime Video's robust mindshare where:

  • "47% of users stream at least 2–3 times per week,
  • 15% watch daily, and
  • 89% stream weekly or more."

With eMarketer already expecting CTV ad sales to grow from $28.79B in 2024 to $46.89B in 2028, expanding at a CAGR of +12.9%, and "98.4% of those dollars going to video ads, solidifying its place as one of the fastest-growing major ad channels in the US," I am of the opinion that AMZN's advertising monetization may remain robust moving forward.

Decelerating Growth Meets Reasonable Valuations

The Consensus Forward Estimates

As a result of the numerous growth levers across commerce, AWS, and advertising, amongst others, it is unsurprising that AMZN has already offered an excellent FQ4'25 revenue guidance of $209.5B at the midpoint (+16.2% QoQ/+11.5% YoY) and operating income guidance of $23.5B (+35% QoQ/+10.8% YoY).

These numbers do not appear overly aggressive as well, based on the YTD outperformance at +11.8% YoY and +32% YoY, respectively.

These reasons may also be why the consensus have been raising their forward estimates, with AMZN expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +11.4%/+19.7% through FY2027, with it underscoring its profitable growth cadence.

This is despite the notable deceleration from the 5Y historical growth profile at +17.9%/+36.9% and the 9Y historical growth at +21.9%/+64.4% (the period that AMZN is profitable), respectively.

AMZN Valuations

As a result of the maturing growth profile and the consequently downgraded P/E valuations from the prior 5Y mean of 167.70x, AMZN has seemingly found a floor at the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 33.34x, near to its 1Y mean of 33.87x.

Thanks to the moderation, AMZN remains rather reasonable at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.69x as well (based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 33.34x and the the consensus 3Y adj EPS growth estimates of +19.7%), compared to the 5Y mean of 4.54x, the sector median of 1.64x, and its peers, including:

  • commerce/advertising - Sea Limited (SE) at 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.55x (using the same methodology),
  • commerce/advertising - Mercado Libre (MELI) at 1.63x,
  • commerce/advertising - Shopify Inc. (SHOP) at 4.42x,
  • commerce/hyperscaler/advertising - Alibaba (BABA) at 2.41x,
  • hyperscaler/advertising - Microsoft (MSFT) at 1.78x,
  • hyperscaler/advertising - Alphabet (GOOG) at 1.58x, and
  • advertising - Meta (META) at 1.99x.

These developments underscore that AMZN is an even better buy after the moderation of the prior rally post FQ3'25 earnings call.

AMZN Remains A Great Buy

AMZN 5Y Stock Price

For now, AMZN has finally broken out of its prior sideways trading after the blowout FQ3'25 earnings call, with the stock charting new heights of $250s while running away from the 50/100/200 day moving averages.

Based on the LTM adj EPS performance at $6.72 (+43.8% sequentially) and the 1Y P/E mean of 33.87x, it is apparent that the stock is still trading near to my fair value price target of $227.60, based on the current stock prices of $234.60.

Based on the consensus raised FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $9.48, there remains an excellent upside potential of +36.7% to my long-term price target of $321 as well.

AMZN Technical Indicators

AMZN's technical indicators appear somewhat reasonable across the RSIs and the trading volumes, with the recent AI-related market-wide meltdown already triggering an improved margin of safety for those looking to add.

This is especially since the October 2025 breakout implies a potential bull-run similar to those observed in 2018, 2020, and 2023-2025, thanks to its robust fundamental performance across AWS cloud computing, Advertising, and commerce, amongst others.

As a result of the multi-pronged growth drivers, I am reiterating my buy rating for the AMZN stock.

Based on the stock's ongoing retest of the second support lines of $230s, interested readers may want to observe the price movement for a little longer before adding, since a further pullback to the established base support line/200 day moving averages of $215s may trigger an expanded long-term return profile.

Given the potential -8.3% downside from current levels, some patience may be more prudent in the mean time.

data.title: IREN Vs. Cipher: The Former Looks Less Promising
data.description:
Introduction

I noticed that comparison articles are becoming more popular, especially now, when we're witnessing a new germination of AI-enablers and, at the same time, a major shift in old business models. Every company is trying to establish itself in the new hyper-high-growth niches within the aggregate AI addressable market, so it's interesting to pick wisely among multiple emerging alternatives. That's why I decided to compare IREN Limited (IREN) with Cipher Mining (CIFR). Both were mainly Bitcoin (BTC) miners before, but they're now rapidly transitioning toward neocloud and data‑center capacity operators. They have their own distinctions in terms of operating activities, but it's interesting to compare their business model shifts so that we can pick the best-in-class miner-to-AI converter for the long haul.

My View On IREN Limited

IREN initially started as a renewable‑powered Bitcoin miner, and they still make over 96% of sales from mining (as of September 2025), but when the AI started to make moves in recent years, they decided to use their renewable energy advantage and existing racks to capitalize on the new theme. Their sites in British Columbia and Texas are powered 100% by clean or renewable energy, and they own/control land with substations and complete data‑center infrastructure. They run a vertically integrated system, so they have more control over costs, and they can theoretically repurpose capacity away from mining toward AI relatively smoothly.

Back in January 2025, the management guided in its update that the mining segment's longer‑term targets 57 EH/s by H2 2025, which should have been supported by a 750 MW build‑out at Childress in Texas and fleet upgrades with Bitmain S21 Pro and S21 XP miners, targeting sub‑15 J/TH efficiency and Childress power costs around 2.8c/kWh on a net basis. Then, in March 2025, they secured ~2.75GW for data centers built in West Texas with a new 600 MW grid connection agreement, and then later in the same month, IREN stated that it would "pause all further Bitcoin Mining expansion upon completion of its 52 EH/s buildout."

By mid-April, they reached 40 EH/s, then raised additional capital "to fund the cost of entering into the prepaid forward transaction" in June 2025. They finally reached the mid-year target of 50 EH/s by the end of June, and started to expand their AI Cloud with thousands of new Nvidia Blackwell GPUs again and again, doubling capacity to 23k GPUs by the end of September. Then the management signed a new multi-year AI cloud contract with a customer, who was willing to secure demand for 11k of their 23k GPUs, which was going to bring in ~$250 million in AI Cloud ARR by the year-end. In mid-October, they priced another convertible notes offering, raised $1 billion at 0% (42.5% conversion premium, due 2031), and then secured $9.7 billion AI Cloud contract with Microsoft (MSFT) in early November.

IREN is moving extremely fast in its business transition, and there has been so much corporate news in just a few months, staying on track to deploy 140k GPUs by the end of 2026 and reach $3.4 billion in AI Cloud ARR (they had only 1,896 NVIDIA H100/H200 GPUs in early 2025 for comparison). By Q1 2026, the plan is to reach $500 in ARR, and then move from that point up to $3.4 billion, which is effectively a 6.8x expansion in less than 12 months.

The deal with MSFT is viewed by market participants differently, depending on their particular take on what comes next for the AI story. For some skeptics, the fact that IREN will have to spend about $8.8 billion in CAPEX to deliver 200 MW of AI compute in exchange for ~$10 billion of revenue over 5 years looks economically unfounded. Even if they reach 85% in EBITDA margin, the ROIC is going to be close to zero or only slightly positive. As one of X's users calculated, there will be a capital shortfall of over $2 billion that will need to be filled with additional debt or equity, and given that they were quite active in debt financing this year, the odds for equity-based financing, and hence dilution, are pretty high. On the other hand, the capacity they're building is essential to get as much of the future market share as possible, and even if they earn 0 in real profits from that MSFT deal, the scale built out over the next few years isn't going anywhere.

As the company jumps from a ~2 MW air‑cooled cluster to a 200 MW liquid‑cooled GB300 deployment over the next years, the execution risks are going to rise, but we should think of the opportunity that will likely come along with that extensive expansion. In growth stories like this, the stock price of an underlying company usually follows the revenue trajectory, and that's why the massive upticks in revenue revisions in recent months are outweighing the risks (at least for now).

My View On Cipher Mining

Cipher Mining is also an ex-pure-play Bitcoin miner, but it's progressing toward a power‑rich digital infrastructure developer to date. They initially went public through a SPAC structure to secure cheap power in West Texas, build large-scale and modular data centers further translate that into low all‑in BTC production costs, which is almost the path of IREN back in the day.

Cipher acquired and developed several Texas sites (Odessa, Black Pearl/Wink, plus JV sites like Alborz, Bear, Chief), energizing ~477 MW and ramping self‑mining capacity to ~23.6 EH/s, which put it in the top tier of global miners. But then the management realized that grid‑connected power is the real value AI needs, they began their strategic pivot to AI/HPC. The main thing for that pivot is Barber Lake in Texas, which is a 300 MW+ site with a new substation and permits already in place.

Their hosting capacity is in great demand from large hyperscalers right now. A few weeks ago, they signed Fluidstick and Google (GOOG) under the new 10-year agreement, which is going to bring in at least $3 billion (up to $7 billion) in contracted revenues for Cipher's 168 MW capacity. The economics here are very different from Cipher's legacy business: rather than volatile BTC revenue tied to price and difficulty, this is recurring streams, which gives CIFR better earnings visibility. What's also noteworthy - Google even took a 5.4% stake in CIFR as part of the deal. After Google, Amazon chose Cipher for powering some of its AI workloads through a 15-year lease agreement.

This quarter, we executed a pivotal transaction with Fluidstack and Google, which firmly established our credibility in the HPC space... we've now taken another major step forward... we've executed a second landmark HPC transaction, this time with Amazon Web Services.

Source: Seeking Alpha Earnings Call Insights

Effectively, CIFR's contracted AI hosting capacity went up from 0 in Q2 2025 to 544 MW as of Q3.

Taken together, the Google/Fluidstack and AWS contracts give Cipher at least ~$8.5 billion of long‑dated and mostly fixed‑rent revenue backlog (before Google's possible extensions) on 468 MW of AI/HPC capacity, all scheduled to start layering in from late 2026. But they need to finance this build-out somehow, which is why we see them attracting new notes offering (although at less favorable terms if we compare the issuance characteristics to IREN's recent issuance).

The business model run by Cipher differs from IREN's because the former is more of a "landlord" than a GPU cloud platform provider. The core HPC model is about developing data‑center infrastructure, then leasing that capacity (power, space, cooling, interconnect) on long‑term contracts, whereas IREN is buying and owning the GPUs and selling compute to its customers. So, IREN's revenue line is tied to GPU capacity and usage, not just rent on four walls and megawatts.

The deals CIFR signed recently, and the backlog they got is set to kick in from late 2026. Today, CIFR's profit and loss statement still looks mining‑centric because, as of Q3, they made ~$72 million in revenue (+64.6% YoY) with 629 BTC mined and 1,500 BTC held. I liked to see them narrowing the GAAP loss rapidly - in the latest quarter, we can see only a $3 million loss compared to a negative $41 million in adjusted loss last year. And the management has no plans to convert profitable sites like Odessa to HPC until at least 2027, which may be reasonable, as this approach protects cash flow generation (at least to some extent).

As they bring in new HPC DCs to live, their revenue is set to rise dramatically over the next few years, according to the current consensus. The revenue consensus revisions look similar to what we saw in IREN's case:

And I don't doubt that their top line is likely to outpace these projections amid new prospective contracts and the current contracts' capacity extensions. The return on the capital they invest isn't at risk to the extent I described it for IREN, because of the business models' difference. Cipher's CAPEX is basically land with substations, high‑voltage lines, shells, cooling plants, and campus infrastructure, which is combined a 20-30 year assets with clear residual value. That value isn't depreciating as fast as IREN's AI cloud assets, like GPUs. So, that's why I think that CIFR's economic IRRs will likely be materially better than what IREN can get out of its Microsoft project.

IREN Vs. Cipher - Valuation

The valuation side looks very interesting. CIFR and IREN are basically identically valued on the FWD P/E basis, but IREN is twice cheaper compared to CIFR in terms of the FWD P/S ratio:

As I've already noted above, Cipher's landlord-like business model is structurally designed to generate decent infrastructure‑style returns on long‑lived assets, whereas IREN's recent deals intentionally traded away project‑level returns to buy credibility and scale in GPU cloud. But IREN competes with already well-established players like CoreWeave (CRWV) and fast-growing Nebius (NBIS), so I assume that they're going to lose on margins because of the pricing pressure going forward, especially given that they've paused developing mining operations. That's why we see the consensus foreseeing deterioration on the bottom line by 2029 (it's likely to come earlier than that, in my view):

For CIFR, we don't have clear visibility because of the lack of investment coverage from big banks, but based on what we have, in FY2026, the EPS should grow by over 44% YoY:

This is where CIFR becomes cheaper than IREN: On the 2-year forward basis, CIFR is valued at less than 30x earnings for 44% YoY EPS growth, while IREN is valued at 38x for 9.54% YoY EPS expansion. I think that if both stocks trade at a PEG of 1.5x next year (close to the sector's norms), then IREN would have a large downside of over 62%, while CIFR will have an upside of over 120%.

Summary Thesis

I believe that the fact that Cipher shouldn't see GPU price swings and a lot of the tech obsolescence risk ahead that IREN is explicitly taking onto its own balance sheet, the market should be willing to overpay for it in terms of forward valuation. In other words, the lower hardware risks that CIFR holds should theoretically give it a higher valuation multiple ahead. Yes, CIFR and IREN aren't the same in the way they're trying to capitalize on the ongoing AI transition, but in terms of risk/reward, CIFR stands out to me, and that's why I'm rating it a "Buy", while rating IREN as a "Hold".

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: GRNJ: First Look At Tom Lee's New Small/Mid Cap Granny Shots ETF
data.description:
Introducing The Fundstrat Granny Shots US Small & Mid Cap ETF

Credit to Tom Lee - he's striking while the iron's hot. Tuesday marked the first day of trading for the Tidal Trust III - Fundstrat Granny Shots US Small- & Mid-Cap ETF (GRNJ), which was presumably created to capitalize on the incredible success of the $3.6B Fundstrat Granny Shots US Large Cap ETF (GRNY), which I reviewed last month. I was excited to get the email announcing the launch of trading of this new fund in a category I feel is underserved, so I loaded GRNJ's 52 holdings into my ETF database, made some calculations and comparisons, and now I'd like to present to you what I believe GRNJ does well and why it might earn a spot in your portfolio. I hope you enjoy the read, and as always, I look forward to the discussion afterward.

Overview

The Granny Shots Strategy

According to its website, GRNJ's investment process

"selects a portfolio of small- and mid-cap stocks aligned with key themes identified by the Portfolio Managers. This approach integrates a top-down fundamental research methodology with a quantitative, bottom-up stock selection strategy. Stocks that align with at least two of the identified themes are included in the Fund, with positions equal-weighted and rebalanced quarterly."

That last part caught my eye. Unlike GRNY, which reconstitutes monthly, GRNJ's portfolio changes quarterly, so I'm assuming this will result in less portfolio turnover. Still, the basic philosophy appears to be the same. Based on the graphic below, GRNY holds 29 stocks based, in part, on their style tilts, and 23 based, in part, on PMI Recovery. According to this section of the Granny Shots website, the strategy

"identifies a relationship between yield-curve dynamics that implies the latter has bottomed out, and seeks to gain exposure to companies benefitting from the forecasted PMI recovery."

Those are short-term themes, but GRNJ also holds stocks based on long-term themes covering energy and cybersecurity, millennials, global labor suppliers, and easing financial conditions. I believe stocks in these sections are more likely to be longer-term holds, but time will tell if that assumption is correct.

The above image is slightly outdated as of August 14, 2025, but it's worth reiterating that all stocks are initially equal-weighted. Put differently, companies satisfying four themes, like BWX Technologies (BWXT), won't receive higher weights - the threshold for inclusion is only two themes.

A Quick Word About Fees and Overlap

GRNJ has the same 0.75% expense ratio as GRNY, so it's quite an expensive fund to own. However, in fairness to Tom Lee, he's about as far from a closet-Indexer as you'll find, so arguably, it's worth it to pay for something different, especially if it can deliver outsized gains. I think that's why GRNY has been so successful, so quickly - word has spread that it's outperforming many large-cap growth benchmarks without much influence from the big tech stocks that have powered the market these last five years.

My analysis reveals GRNJ shares the same unique features as GRNY. To illustrate, I compared its overlap with about 250 U.S. small- and mid-cap ETFs and found it averaged only 5%, which is incredibly low. Although subject to change, GRNJ is most similar to the Invesco S&P MidCap Momentum ETF (XMMO), and even in this case, overlap is less than 20%. You can run your own custom overlap analysis here, but I think you'll find GRNJ complements your existing assets well.

Analysis

GRNY Sector Allocations

One potential downside to GRNJ is that it's highly concentrated at the sector level. Currently, it allocates about two-thirds to just Industrials and Technology combined, as summarized below.

  • Industrials: 38.52%
  • Technology: 24.89%
  • Materials: 7.74%
  • Financials: 7.61%
  • Communication Services: 5.79%
  • Consumer Discretionary: 5.74%
  • Energy: 3.87%
  • Health Care: 3.84%
  • Utilities: 1.98%.

GRNJ Peer Group and Fundamental Analysis

Next, I'd like to dive into GRNJ's fundamentals, as I think this will provide some nice insight into what the fund offers compared to other small/mid-cap funds. As comparators, I've chosen the following four ETFs:

  • Vanguard Small-Cap Growth ETF (VBK)
  • Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth ETF (VOT)
  • Janus Henderson Small/Mid Cap Growth ETF (JSMD)
  • Congress SMID Growth ETF (CSMD).

The following table highlights selected fundamental metrics for GRNJ's top 25 holdings, which total 49.15%. Please have a scan of the list and the summary metrics at the bottom for each ETF, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

Takeaway #1: High 1.49 Five-Year Portfolio Beta

One standout statistic is GRNJ's 1.49 five-year portfolio beta, which is the weighted average result of its underlying holdings. While not all volatility should be treated negatively (especially in the current environment), a backtest reveals that an equal-weight portfolio of these stocks would have performed just as badly as VBK and VOT in 2022.

That's not to say GRNJ will experience these same losses, since it will have the opportunity to rotate into more defensive companies through short-term themes, including style tilt. However, I simply want to point out that for the time being, GRNJ seems quite risky, and it's unknown how the Granny Shots team will react in risk-off environments.

Takeaway #2: GRNJ Has Huge Momentum Factor Exposure

One thing I appreciate about the Granny Shots strategy is that it leans heavily on the momentum factor. I'm not sure if it's temporary or not, but we see that GRNJ's holdings have seen their share prices increase by 64.07% over the last year, compared to only 29.71% and 22.52% for VBK and VOT, the two broad-based passive growth funds. JSMD is closest, with its holdings gaining 42.88% on a weighted average basis, but still, GRNJ has some of the best momentum features among all the small/mid-cap ETFs I track.

Takeaway #3: GRNJ Trades At A Reasonably Attractive Valuation

GRNJ trades at 23.36x forward earnings, which, except for JSMD, is actually pretty reasonable compared to its peers. The table below highlights several other common valuation metrics, including its 17.47x trailing P/CF ratio and its 3.21x trailing EV/Sales ratio, both of which are on the low end. Overall, GRNJ receives a 3.99/10 Value Score in my ETF Rankings System, and although that score isn't great on its surface, it's solid for the earnings growth that it offers, which is upwards of 20% on a one-year estimated basis.

Takeaway #4: Margins Aren't Great, But Debt Is Low

The fundamental analysis table highlights GRNJ's 8.62% weighted average EBIT margins, which are lower than VBK's and about half of VOT's. Net margins look slightly better, but JSMD and CSMD do much better on the quality factor while keeping in the same size segment - their weighted average market caps are only $7.5B and $13.6B, respectively.

For a more detailed look at quality using only GRNJ's top ten holdings, please consider the table below, which also shows JSMD and CSMD leading on Gross and EBITDA margins, ROA, ROE, ROTC, and Asset Turnover.

However, GRNJ's holdings use substantially less leverage, evidenced by its 0.26 and 0.68 LTD/Total Capital and Debt/Equity ratios, so I can see how it could perform better in higher-rate environments. I should also note that JSMD's higher 29.10% ROE comes from its higher leverage, since that's one of the ratio's three components, along with net margin and asset turnover.

Investment Recommendation

GRNJ doesn't have any track record yet, so I'm staying neutral for this initial coverage. However, I can confidently say it offers excellent momentum factor exposure not commonly found in the small/mid-cap size segment, and its components don't appear to be excessively overpriced, especially when factoring in the high earnings growth they offer. GRNJ's holdings also have low leverage on average, which might help offset some of the risks of its concentrated high-beta strategy. Still, with quarterly rebalances, there will be enough opportunities to adjust based on market conditions.

In terms of competition, GRNJ could prove to be a nice alternative to VBK and VOT, two broad-based small- and mid-cap growth funds. However, I'll be watching to see if GRNJ can outperform JSMD and CSMD when momentum falls out of favor, as they appear to have stronger factor mixes at the moment. If it can, I think that will be a testament to the Granny Shots strategy, and would likely lead to a Buy rating sometime in 2026.

I hope you enjoyed this "first look" at GRNJ, and as always, I look forward to any questions you might have in the comments below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Firan Technology Is A Speculative Bet On Defense And Airbus' Growth
data.description:
Firan Technology Group Corporation (OTCPK:FTGFF) is an aerospace electronics supplier of defense and commercial aviation. FTGFF operates ten facilities across North America and Asia, with a new site under development in India. FTGFF focuses on advanced manufacturing rather than general consumer electronics. Concretely, this means they manufacture printed circuit boards (PCBs), with high-density interconnect (HDI) and semi-additive process (SAP). This way, its products can capture the rising demand for lighter, more complex avionics systems. Recently, FTGFF also acquired FLYHT Aerospace, which expanded its data-connectivity solutions that help it provide content for Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF). And, as a whole, I believe FTGFF is well-positioned to benefit from broader secular tailwinds tied to the US spending on defense applications.

Aviation Electronics

Firan Technology Group Corporation is a high-technology manufacturing company that produces aerospace and defense electronics. It was founded back in 1983 under the name Helix Circuit and later renamed itself as Circuit World Corporation. After a reverse takeover in 2003, the company became Firan Technology Group. FTGFF is currently headquartered in Toronto, Ontario, and operates ten manufacturing facilities worldwide. Its footprint includes sites in Canada, the US, China, plus a new upcoming location under construction in India.

As a quick overview, FTGFF has two operating segments: 1) FTG Circuits and 2) FTG Aerospace. First, FTG Circuits produces high-technology printed circuit boards (PCBs) for high-reliability systems, not general consumer electronics. Its PCBs are high-density interconnect (HDI), RF circuitry, thermal management boards, rigid and flex assembly PCBs, and semi-additive process PCBs. This segment serves aviation, defense, space, and high-tech industries, and uses FTGFF's production sites in the US (California, Massachusetts, Minnesota, and Virginia), Canada (Toronto), and China (Tianjin).

As for its other segment, FTG Aerospace develops high-reliability avionics hardware for human-machine interfaces (HMI). For example, FTGFF's HMIs are used in back-lit control panels and assemblies, keyboards, multipurpose control and display units (MCDUs), cursor control devices, bezels, and line-replaceable units (LRUs). This division has manufacturing facilities in the US (California), Canada (Toronto), and China (Tianjin).

With that in mind, FTGFF is now investing in new technologies, such as HDI and SAP PCB processes. Essentially, they want to improve their technology and deliver more circuitry in a smaller area. HDI features a much higher wiring density per unit area compared to standard PCBs. This is why HDIs are typically used in applications where space, weight, and signal integrity are critical, even in harsh environments. SAP is a PCB manufacturing technique that enables ultra-fine circuit patterns using narrower traces and tighter spacing. So, by furthering its HDI and SAP processes, FTGFF should be able to increasingly improve its value proposition.

It's also worth mentioning that last December, FTGFF acquired FLYHT in a transaction valued at $23 million. At the time of the acquisition, FLYHT represented a $20 million increase in annualized sales for FTGFF. Additionally, FLYHT expanded FTGFF's footprint into commercial aerospace aftermarket systems, which is a high-margin niche. This M&A transaction also strengthened FTGFF's ties to Airbus because it provides content for its Airbus aircraft.

Besides, acquiring FLYHT gave FTGFF new technology lines for backup aircraft communication and telemetry systems. This know-how is critical as it's used on all Airbus aircraft (Satcom via Iridium Satellite), a 5G-enabled data recorder transmitting flight and maintenance data, and an aircraft-mounted sensor transmitting high-altitude atmospheric data.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, FTGFF trades at a $190.1 million market cap. Its most recent financials from Q3 2025 show its balance sheet holds only $7.1 million in cash and equivalents. They also have approximately $14.0 million in financial debt (aside from their regular operating liabilities). So, I estimate FTGFF's EV at roughly $197.0 million. And according to Seeking Alpha's dashboard on FTGFF, they're projected to generate $149.7 million in revenues by 2026. This suggests a reasonably cheap forward EV/S of 1.3, which is lower than its sector's median forward EV/S of 3.4.

I also think FTGFF is a reasonably profitable operation. They generated $4.0 million in operating income during their last quarter, implying an annualized EBIT of around $16.0 million. This would put FTGFF at an EV/EBIT ratio of 12.3, which again is considerably cheaper than its peers' EV/EBIT of 25.4. Lastly, I calculate that FTGFF generated roughly $4.0 million in cash flow during Q3 2025. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its cash flows from operations and CAPEX, which suggests this should be a self-sustainable company at this point.

Furthermore, looking forward, FTGFF has a few growth tailwinds. First, management noted that demand in the aerospace and defense sectors should continue increasing in the coming years, in particular with the development of new programs with next-generation aircraft and defense systems. FTGFF's investor presentation also mentioned they intent to continue acquiring new technology (like they did with FLYHT). Plus, FTGFF intends to maximize its capacity across its facilities in Canada, the US, China, and now India while adding inorganic growth through targeted new acquisitions.

It's also worth mentioning that FTGFF is tightly linked to the two dominant aerospace behemoths, Airbus and Boeing (BA). FTGFF supplies components to OEMs like these. So, if their business grows, then FTGFF's revenues are also likely to increase. In that sense, Airbus and Boeing both have a favorable demand outlook. FTGFF's projects that post-pandemic (2020 to 2021) deliveries are starting to recover. Airbus outperforms Boeing and should keep its lead through 2026. Airbus has a larger order pipeline with 8,716 aircraft compared to 6,531 orders from Boeing. However, Boeing will rebound in 2025, with shipments projected to be 50% higher than in 2024.

Similarly, the broader US defense sector is an even larger secular tailwind for FTGFF. In fact, the US has consistently increased its defense spending in recent years, and its 2026 budget request is now close to $1 trillion. And ultimately, higher defense budgets translate to more aircraft and avionics upgrades, and electronics procurement, which are all core areas for FTGFF. Hence, all in all, it's difficult to be bearish on FTGFF, especially since it trades at a very compelling valuation with promising growth prospects.

Naturally, please keep in mind this is a microcap with thin trading liquidity. Its average trading volume is roughly 2,216 shares on the Nasdaq (NDAQ), which at the current $7.50 PPS indicates only $16.6 thousand in trading liquidity. So, ideally, I would avoid buying the OTC shares available in the US because even tiny buy/sell orders can lead to significant slippage. But even its consolidated trading volume is low at 37.3 thousand shares, suggesting a combined trading liquidity (across all exchanges) of just $279.8 thousand. Thus, given its microcap status and thin liquidity, I think you should expect substantial volatility with this stock.

Conclusion: Worth Speculating

Overall, I think FTGFF is a relatively safe long-term bet in the aerospace defense sector. In theory, it should benefit from the increasing US defense spending through its clients like Airbus, which are large defense OEMs. Its valuation also looks compelling, and it has secular tailwinds that I believe will last for the foreseeable future, given the rising geopolitical tensions worldwide. Having said that, FTGFF remains a microcap with thin liquidity, so it's undoubtedly a much more speculative bet. It's also a somewhat small player in its niche, making it a bit riskier than other, much larger component suppliers. Yet, on balance, I still feel a speculative "Buy" is fair at these levels for long-term investors. Just make sure you size your position accordingly.

data.title: AMG GW&K Municipal Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Drivers

  • ​GW&K’s Municipal Bond Fund slightly underperformed the Bloomberg 10-Year Municipal Bond Index for the quarter.
  • ​Municipal bonds posted strong returns in the third quarter, led by September’s sharp move at the long end. July and August returns were dominated by curve steepening rather than broad price gains, with retail demand pinned to the front end while value built further out the curve.
  • ​Broader rates also fell over the quarter, though only by single digits. A weaker labor picture gave the Federal Reserve Board (the Fed) room to cut by 25 basis points in September, but an upgraded outlook for growth and inflation kept a more aggressive rally in check.
  • ​Heading into the fourth quarter, markets are pricing in a slightly more aggressive easing path than the Fed’s own projections, but a range of crosscurrents will determine how that plays out.

Top Contributors/Detractors

  • ​We continued a year-long trend of heavy trading activity in the third quarter as we looked to take advantage of the sharp steepening of the municipal bond yield curve.

Contributors

  • ​An overweight to bonds longer than the Index was the biggest positive as rates declined.

Detractors

  • ​An allocation to shorter maturities than the Index detracted from performance.

Positioning Update

  • ​On the sell side, we focused on short-callable bonds with limited upside, particularly high-quality names that had run their course.
  • ​We redeployed mostly in the 10–13-year area of the curve where the yield and roll created some of the most compelling expected return profiles in over a decade.
  • ​The extension trades provided roughly 100 basis points of nominal yield pickup, with roll dynamics adding another 100-150 basis points.

Outlook

  • ​Municipal bonds enter the fourth quarter in excellent shape. Nominal yields remain well above their 10- and 20-year averages, and the curve is still steep enough to offer meaningful expected return from roll.
  • ​Ratios at the front end are stretched, leaving less cushion if broader yields back up, but value persists in intermediate and longer maturities, where roll and carry do more of the work.
  • ​Credit fundamentals remain sound. States continue to budget conservatively and maintain elevated reserves, even as some have begun modest draws on rainy-day funds.
